annotations.label: 22/7 exceeds π

anchor.snippet: Proofs of the mathematical result that the rational number

annotations.note: Mathlib has numerical bounds like `Real.pi_lt_d4 : π < 3.1416` which imply 22/7 > π, but no statement of `π < 22/7` by name.

annotations.label: 22/7 as Diophantine approximation of π

anchor.snippet: is a widely used Diophantine approximation of

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion identifying 22/7 as a named Diophantine approximation of π.

annotations.label: Archimedes' proof via 96-gon

anchor.snippet: Archimedes wrote the first known proof

annotations.note: No formalization of Archimedes' polygonal bounds on π is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dalzell's integral proof

anchor.snippet: The proof first devised by British electrical engineer Donald Percy Dalzell

annotations.note: Dalzell's specific integral identity ∫₀¹ x⁴(1-x)⁴/(1+x²) dx = 22/7 - π is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integral is positive

anchor.section: Details of evaluation of the integral
anchor.snippet: That the integral is positive follows from the fact that the integrand is non-negative

annotations.note: The general principle is `intervalIntegral.integral_nonneg`, but no specific instance for the Dalzell integrand is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Evaluation of the integral

anchor.section: Details of evaluation of the integral
anchor.snippet: It remains to show that the integral in fact evaluates to the desired quantity

annotations.note: The closed-form evaluation ∫₀¹ x⁴(1-x)⁴/(1+x²) dx = 22/7 - π is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dalzell quick upper and lower bounds

anchor.snippet: if 1 is substituted for x in the denominator, one gets a lower bound on the integral

annotations.note: The specific upper/lower bounds 1/1260 ≤ 22/7 − π ≤ 1/630 are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 355/113 exceeds π

anchor.snippet: the well-known Diophantine approximation and far better upper estimate

annotations.note: `Real.pi_lt_d20 : π < 3.14159265358979323847` is sharp enough to imply 355/113 > π, but the statement is not stated explicitly.

annotations.note: The specific quantitative bounds on 355/113 − π via the corresponding integral are not formalized.
